reviews.review_text:
The Witcher 2 is an amazing game for something released back in 2011. In many ways it feels more thoughtfully designed than the next entry in the series

The gameplay here is genuinely good, sure there are bad aspects, like being able to drink potions only during meditation, meaning you cannot heal/use heal potion mid fight but it never bothered me, health regeneration outside combat is pretty fast. In hindsight the system actually encourages preparation before battles, which fits the fantasy of being a witcher, but it can be annoying yeah

Most importantly for me the game truly understands what a witcher is supposed to be. In my opinion a witcher is a bounty hunter who relies equally on swords and basic magic. CDPR understood this far better in Witcher 2 than they did in Witcher 3

In Witcher 3 even if you build Geralt as a hybrid swordsman mage(like I did) combat usually devolves into spamming sword attacks with the occasional use of Aard or Igni, the other signs like Yrden, Quen or Axii are either situational or unnecessary most of the time

The Witcher 2 does the opposite. The game constantly pushes you to use Geralt's full arsenal. Enemy types, arenas and combat mechanics are designed in a way that gives every sign a purpose. On top of that the progression system never completely breaks the balance between the signs or shifts the entire combat system toward one side. Because of that the combat feels far more "witcher like". You genuinely feel like a professional monster hunter using every tool available to you instead of just swinging a sword nonstop

The story and world design are best discussed together because in my opinion they heavily influence one another. Unlike The Witcher 3, where the world is massive and filled with hundreds of question marks, The Witcher 2 features smaller but filled with more refined content. There is less content overall yes but the content that is there feels handcrafted and is genuinely good

I also preferred the quests in Witcher 2 over those in Witcher 3. Your choices actually feel meaningful. It is not on the level of Cyberpunk 2077, where quests can hit you with uncomfortable moral dilemmas, hit emotionally or make you rethink something, but for an RPG from that era the level of branching is impressive to this day

Most importantly the consequences of your decisions are visible. Around the middle of the game the story literally splits into two fully developed routes with different locations, characters and events. It is not the illusion of choice it is essentially an entirely different playthrough and this is what makes The Witcher 2 great. Choices in Witcher 3 mainly affect small details, individual scenes or changing small things in the ending. The overall journey stays mostly the same, while in Witcher 2 your decisions feel far more immediate and up close because you see the consequences almost instantly, and they directly affect your gameplay and story rather than only at the very end of the game

The only major weakness of the game is the main story itself, which feels like a large filler episode between more important events. The plot works more as a standalone Geralt adventure and setup for the coming war. I think Geralt's personal struggles and conflicts could and should have been explored more(though maybe I simply messed up with my choices during playthrough). While the story is still good I do not think it reaches the levels of The Witcher 3 in that particular aspect

Still holds up, I liked the experience even better than the first game. 

The story is fantastic and kept me hooked from start to finish. Of course, choices matter here too and because of mine, I will definitely be playing it again soon :) There is everything you can expect from a true Witcher game - political intrigue, betrayal, and difficult choices where there isn't always a clear right answer. The writing remains one of the game's strongest aspects, something expected from CDPR. 

The combat is something that can feel off, but I think one can easily re-adjust and get used to. There are some though battles and I felt that pretty good on dark difficulty, lol. I won’t lie, I died a lot, but when you finally beat that boss the feeling is very rewarding.

There are many aspects in the game, that CDPR looked into and made better than the first game, always good to see this. My only complaint is that The Witcher 2 felt a little short :( I managed to beat it in just 51 hours, not rushing or anything ofc.

In the end I will repeat what I said about the first game – this game deserves more love. In my opinion it is a must play for every Witcher fan out there! You can better your experience with some mods, there are pretty good ones out there :)

This game did not age well, i recently wanted to replay all the games and i beat the first witcher and went on to this one, the combat is clunky and seems to be a step down from the first game, i understand that this was their attempt at making a game using their own engine, but dude, its bad. The only viable way to fight is to spam Quen and roll around like an idiot, bombs do virtually nothing to monsters and humans alike, traps do nothing to monsters and humans alike, the visuals and the story is okay. i would honestly say just skip this game unless you are a masochist.

it's really the most middest of midst but the gameplay is just so boring and tedious my god
other than that (i.e. story, design) it's pretty good

65%
+ Some strong characters and voice acting
+ Enjoyable combat
+ Interesting main story
- Dated gameplay
- Some weak characters
- Some plot points are silly

Review – The Witcher 2: Assassins of Kings Enhanced Edition (Steam)

I played The Witcher 2 after finishing newer RPGs, and despite its age, it still stands out for its story, choices, and political intrigue.

👍 What I Loved

The story is excellent, with a darker and more mature tone than most fantasy RPGs.
Choices actually matter. Entire quest lines, locations, and perspectives can change depending on your decisions.
The characters are memorable, especially Geralt, Roche, Iorveth, and Triss.
The game respects your intelligence and doesn't constantly hold your hand.

👎 What Didn't Age Well

Combat can feel clunky and unforgiving, especially during the early chapters.
The UI and inventory management are dated.
Some quests don't always provide clear directions, which can lead to unnecessary confusion.
The game expects preparation before difficult fights, something that may frustrate players coming from The Witcher 3.

⚖️ Final Verdict
The Witcher 2 may not be as polished or accessible as The Witcher 3, but it offers one of the strongest stories and most meaningful choices in the RPG genre. If you can tolerate some dated mechanics, you'll find a fantastic adventure with consequences that genuinely matter.

Recommended 👍
A must-play for fans of story-driven RPGs and anyone who wants to experience one of Geralt's best adventures. ⭐⭐⭐⭐
enjoyed it thoroughly after playing Witcher 1, only wish it was longer highly recommend

I really tried to like this, I really did. 

Dialogues are interesting.
Mini games are horrible.
Understading game mechanics pretty much requires googling.
Combat is just awful and unresponsive.
All women cat-walk and have tits out because average video game.
Movement, at least on gamepad, is quite literally broken and snaps directions.
Lock-on system gets blocked by a little bit of grass sticking out of ground.

I put myself through a lot before abandoning games, and this has pushed me too far.

Especially mow I have the PC to run this game with all the bells and whistles to 11 it is such a beautiful game. The attention to detail in everything is amazing and far above average. Okay Witcher 3 is even bigger and better. Still this game deserves MUCH more respect than it is getting. It's a gem of a fantasy world that feels far more alive than most.
If you  skip this then you are missing something quite special.

I dont know if things have changed since I got the game or if it wasnt an issue for most people, but for me at least, it was ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ impossible to get the game to actually work. like genuinely it took like a week of looking through obscure forum posts for solutions to finally get the game to actually launch. then when I actually got the launcher to launch the game itself still didnt launch and took a couple more hours to figure out that it was an issue with my CPU having too many cores and I had to fix it by ending an incredibly specific task in task manager each time I started the game up.
It's a solid game overall, nowhere near as good as 3 but it has its moments and I like the more political style of story, even with the plotholes and shortcomings. the combat is the clunkiest ♥♥♥♥ ever and kind of a massive pain in the ass sometimes. the graphics hold up well enough, the technical quality is kinda awful but the lighting and artstyle carries it. overall I had a fun time and if you're a big fan of the franchise like me then I recommend you give it a shot.
one of the coolest things this game does, in my opinion, is having a completely different second act based on who you sided with in the first act. and when I say completely different I mean COMPLETELY different; different characters, different quests(both main + side), different locations.
And my god CDPR for the love of O' Dimm please fix your freaking game.
I dont actually remember what I did to fix the game originally, but I think it involved installing a really old version of the Visual C++ redistributables from like 2010.

Oh, and be warned DO NOT make too many saveslots, if you make too many the game's performance will start tanking and eventually it'll just start crashing randomly, and the more saveslots you make the more often it crashes, eventually it got so bad that I couldnt even play the game for more than a couple minutes at a time. then, once you go to start clearing your saveslots the game will just crash after deleting like 3 or so of them for some reason.

Its nice, a bit linear when compared with the other two games and its smaller, lacks some of the open world of the first and third game and some of its rpg elements, its a good game, but its the worst of the series.

So much good stuff to say. Takes what was already great about the first game and expands, improves, and polishes everything. This game looks absolutley gorgeous, even by today's standard. Like genuinly, the fact that this game was released THREE years after the last one is so insane.
Same goes for the story here! Geralt entering deeply into political turmoil amidst larger than life characters, with the ultimate goal of chasing down a killer? Wow what a hook. Letho absolutely steals the show in every scene that he's in. Same goes for Iorveth. OOOOOOOH my god I'm a Iorveth fan I need to see him in more story beats next game thank you very much!
I do have some complaints though! The game is very very short. That's alright! But especially chapter 3 feels like a blip with barely any side content. Was really hoping there'd be more to Loc Muinne. The side quests are almost non-existent, and though there's some very fun ones like the one with the troll under the bridge and the chicken man, I had hoped they'd just.... I had hoped there'd be more.
And the combat is still kind of goofy. It's a lot better than in the first game, but to account for a slightly better combat system they've also upped the damage of everything. Especially that last boss on the tower, did not have a good time...
Overall, phenomenal experience. Very pretty, and I'm very glad I chose to play the first two games before playing Wild Hunt. Let's see if it lives up to the internet's hype.
Downgrade on story feel and some bits from Witcher 1, upgrade on graphics and gameplay. 7/10

An Incredible Dark Fantasy RPG with Masterful Political Intrigue!
With nearly 30 hours on record, this game proves itself to be a mature, challenging, and narrative-driven classic that beautifully paved the way for The Witcher 3.

Deep Narrative with Meaningful Choices: The political plotlines, royal intrigues, and the hunt for the kingslayer are brilliantly written. The choices you make carry heavy consequences, drastically altering the direction of the story in subsequent chapters.

Tactical and Challenging Combat: You cannot just button-mash your way through encounters. Success requires tactical preparation—utilizing Geralt's potions, blade oils, traps, and magical Signs to survive, especially during intense boss battles and monster hunts.

Gritty and Immersive Atmosphere: The world design, environments, and general art direction still hold up incredibly well. The dark fantasy atmosphere is wonderfully thick, perfectly complemented by a stellar and immersive soundtrack.

A true RPG masterpiece for anyone who loves complex, mature storytelling and rich lore. Highly recommended! 9/10.

walau gamenya udh jadul banget, combat nya kaku, & animasinya kaku tp menurutku ttp asik sii ini game apalagi buat kalian yg mau coba series witcher saranku mulai dari witcher 2 ini... kecuali klo kalian pengin bener2 paham smua alur cerita series witcher & kuat buat main witcher 1 yg udh jadul boleh beli trilogy sekalian. jujur klo yg witcher 1 aku gk terlalu suka karena udh terlalu jadul bgt. saranku wajib main ini dlu sblm main witcher 3 biar gk bingung nanti. jd menurutku worth it to buy

Played this game in 2026 and i absolutely loved it. Having read all The Witcher novels, dozens of comics, and having played The Witcher 3, I can say that this was a fantastic representation of the series and the character Geralt of Rivia. Amazing music, dialogue, and fluid combat are what made this game stand out to me. All these years later, this game plays well and recommend it for others who want to relive 2000's nostalgia or for the die hard Witcher fans like myself who wanted to experience this adventure for the first time.

EXCELLENT TITLE. I've waited many years to be able to play this after putting 1500hrs in w3 on ps4-5. I played w1 first and i will say length could have been longer, even long as w1 for sure. I did feel like this game set up exactly what a player loves in W3 though. Combat is amazing as well. Typically i don't have much fun on mouse and keys BUT this combat was so visually pleasing my little clicks looked like MASSIVE gestures of a Witcher! Signs are great as well, all around great game just needed a longer base storyline!

I've kind of had the inverse experience with playing 2 than the experience I had with 1 (playing both for the first time in 2026): Witcher 2 is much prettier, more dramatic, more cinematic, cooler boss fights, cooler storytelling moments, etc.  I cannot STAND the combat.  I came around to liking the combat in 1 by about 6-8 hours in.  I am well past that in 2 and while I don't find it intolerable, I dislike it.  

The first game felt like a rhythm game with a few strategic/preparation elements added.  2 feels like... a poorly-thought-out mess of way too many hotkeys with no active pause and a riposte mechanic that is infuriatingly opaque.  The tutorial assigned me to Easy, which I accepted (I played 1 in Normal and did just fine), and it's...  eh.  I'm just not having any fun with the combat.  The difficulty spike between Easy and Normal is too high for me to learn how to play on Normal even though I'm bored as hell on Easy.

However, everything ELSE about 2 (okay almost everything: not the pathing.  the pathing and level design are MUCH worse) is better than 1.  

I don't know, this game feels like a retro labor of love to play even more than 1 did.  I'm not *not* having fun with it, and I'd rec it to people who specifically like either the Witcher franchise or early 2010s RPGs or both, but I would NOT recommend it to anyone who's not played anything older than a modern (DA/ME 2 or TES: Skyrim)-era action/adventure RPG previously and liked it.

Generally the drama and the writing are making up for how infuriating the mechanics are.  Play this game if you care about the franchise or are just really, really committed to a certain kind of janky 15+-year-old RPG.

Only took me 12 years to finally beat the game lol

The shift from 1 to 2 is extreme. Graphics are appealing but gameplay loop fell flat to 1. More of a focus on politics rather than monsters. Not my cup of tea but not a bad ride tbh.
works ok in desktop mode after manually setting compatibility, forcing gamepad controls using keyboard

Pretty solid game, but compared to the first witcher, this game is pretty straightfoward which is something i didnt like.

7.5/10

As someone who decided to play Witcher 1 prior to this (would not recommend unless you want to listen to the banger end credits song) I was blown away by this game!!
    Where the first games actions didn't really seem to matter in the end no matter what you decided, it was quite the opposite for this game. I hear the 2nd and 3rd chapters differ greatly depending on weather or not you side with Iorveth or Vernon Roche, which makes me excited to come back at a later date. 
     The game looks and runs really well, and the character design was fantastic, each of the chapters had a distinct and unique style (very much unlike the first). 
    The only real thing i can complain about is the destination tracker was off, which sometimes made it an inconvenience in complex cities like Verden, but that like the most nothing thing like ever.

A truly different game than Witcher 3 and yet special and good in its own way. 
Now I am all the more looking forward to The Witcher 4.

С самого начала прохождения я заприметил достижение на одну жизнь. Перед этим прошел на Темной сложности готовясь к этому испытанию и оттачивая навыки. После того как я привык к большому количеству хп противника, маленькому количеству моего, и отсутствию должной подготовки я думал что на Безумной сложности будет также опасно, как оказалось ничего подобного. Единственное чему угрожала эта игра это моему процессору заставив оставить только 4 ядра, чтобы она хоть как-то запускалась и редко вылетала. Но графика в игре тоже на высшем уровне, хотя к чему здесь проц.

I've played the first game and a couple months later here we are at its sequel, finally started this one. I'm at Act 1 so far (4h in) and plan to edit this review once I beat the game!

So far... Such a massive improvement from the first game, like seriously, I don't even know where to start. The graphics are one thing, better lighting, character models look so much better now, the clothes are cool, random NPCs doesn't look so weird now, they look fine.

Alright, about the thing that bothered me the most back then on Witcher 1: backtracking.

Considering I only finished the prologue and barely started Act 1, the pacing was great, maps are easy to navigate, you walk faster, no enemies blocking your way... backtracking I think never happened (as of yet), it was fine. 

But some things while walking are still clunky as heck, like having to climb a wall you could've jumped over, they did not implement jump in this game which is a shame, really, reeks old because the animation of climbing takes a bit to finish so you end up getting bored at times you have to climb stuff around, specially ladders that take some time for the animation to finish. It's like the game's animations don't keep up with its beautiful graphics.

The combat got revamped, refreshed, it's a totally new experience, leaning heavily towards fast-paced action instead of auto-attacking with one or two buttons for spells like the first game, here you got a whole lot of new actions to perform during battle. For people who like complexity, it's a whole feast. I'm not a big fan, I prefer button mashing + dodging, basic stuff, and that so far works fine too, you can play in many different ways, life is good, enjoy it.

Story so far, eh... I can't tell but the prologue was fine, well written, same quality as in the first game only with much better cutscenes this time so the storytelling feels richer. I'm loving it, I didn't get bored, was following their political worldbuilding without dozing off so yeah, for me it remains really good and interesting, you wish to know more about the characters, what exactly is happening, makes you look forward to what's next and that's the recipe of a good time.

Soundtrack... Mmm, I gotta be critic but I'm in the beginning of the game, I feel like the combat theme is very similar to the first game, kinda reminds me of "hunting" which is what witchers are all about, the theme sounded just like the first game when you enter a battle, so I don't know, wasn't bad but nothing new that caught my attention. Uhh, the Inn I'm at right now has no music, I think? I didn't pay attention whereas the Inn music in Witcher 1 is really good. As for Witcher 2, I'm not paying much attention to the soundtrack as of yet.

In conclusion, I'm enjoying the experience a lot so far! The journey only has begun so for now, I recommend you try it. Once I'm done, I'll try remembering to update this review. Thanks for reading!

[h1] The sequel that couldn't be...or rather the least functional game I ever played [/h1]

Hello everyone, you may call me Sapphire. I just finished two playthroughs of this game on its two main paths (Blue stripes and Squirrels) and thought if anything on my library is deserving of a review, this is it.

[h2] Quick and dirty version[/h2]

[b]The gist:[/b] Let me get this out of the way the story is good, it's everything else around it that's the issue, I'll be honest this game isn't pleasant, if you really wanna see the witcher 2 story just do it on youtube. This game is really janky, badly designed, and worse of all both boring and infuriating.

[b]Performance:[/b] Oh boy- Where do I start? I have a good pc, I can run most new games that come out, and even when I can't run a game on its max settings, all it takes is a quick trip to the graphics settings so sort everything out, not this time, I tried every single setting here, this game will not have a stable frame rate, but worse is the constant crashing and abhorrent save system that made me want to die, this game crashed more than anything else I ran on my pc, actually, nothing else, I played on any platform, has crashed even a tenth as much as this game has.

[h1] - [/h1]

[h2] Long and detailed version[/h2]

[h3]The Positives[/h3]

My review ended up being a few thousand letters too long so I'll quickly summarize the positive things, cause there aren't that many- game looks good, Art direction is great, Story is ok, and there's a couple of fun minigames, yeah that's it.

[h3]The Neutrals[/h3]

[b] Quests:[/b] They're not as good I'm not gonna lie to you, there are some really good ones like Little sisters, but most of the quests in this game are nothing burgers it's really disappointing after W1. and the "Alternate paths" for most quests is just an unsatisfying ending + less gold and xp for you, this game tries way too hard to be a "Choices Matter" game to the point it forces your hand at the finale to either save Triss or help your chosen companion see the end of their quest line, which sucks cause the game has been forcing you to look for Triss and aid your companion of choice this entire time and now it goes "Well you can only see the ending of one of these lol" then it doesn't matter either way cause Triss and your companion both survive so why did the game even bother not letting me do both quests-

[b] Pacing:[/b] The pacing of the main quest is very hit and miss, there are a lot of sections where nothing happens, heck there's chapter 2 with (Curing the poison/Lifting the curse) where you have to finish multiple side quests pretending to be main quests.

[h3]The Negatives[/h3]

[b] Crashing:[/b] This game will crash for anything, any single thing, this game will crash if you finish quest, exit a cutscene, save the ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ game- Whether this game crashes or not is up to its mood and there's nothing you can do about it, I think I had to suffer around a 150ish crashes in order to get these two playthroughs done... Sometimes it takes steam with it, sometimes steam becomes unresponsive and sometimes it black screens my whole pc it's so weird.

[b] The saving system:[/b] This game has a really bad saving system, for starters you can not overwrite saves you have to make new ones, and the game doesn't know how to overwrite saves either, it will keep making new autosaves, first issue I came across is the game game began crashing whenever I tried to load, reason? too many saves, and deleting saves is hard when the game crashes if you delete a save and steam downloads them again if you delete them in your file manage, so I turned off steam cloud, but then whenever I saved, the game would freeze for 5 seconds, I didn't know what to do so I enabled steam cloud and decided to live with the crashes, for a while it worked, until the game began to crash on save, I had to disable and enable steam cloud, that fixed the issue until it happened again later, same solution though no biggie, except the game stopped saving at some point, I tried everything, I couldn't get it to save, so I was forced to run the game from chapter 2 to the end for the last few achievements, and it took me a whole day cause it kept crashing-

[b] The Turorial & Mechanics:[/b]  How often do you come across a bad tutorial? not a tutorial that's boring or annoying or too long, no a tutorial that fails at its job and teaches you diddly squat? When you die in the tutorial arena cause you're not used to the million combat mechanics they boot you back to the main menu instead of letting you try again, however it's worse when you actually start the game and learn all the mechanics in the tutorial are locked behind a skill tree and you can't access most of them- Who the hell designs their tutorials like that? not that the mechanics are any good, this game is bloated with a million mechanics and half of them don't work properly and the other half is locked behind a skill tree, sure teach me how to use throwing knives and parry in the tutorial then lock them behind level ups when the game starts whatever.

[b] Crafting & Inventory:[/b] Oh witcher crafting why do we hurt each other like this? remember how annoying the crafting was in the first game? don't worry they ensured to repeat that, once again, it's a million annoying things, you get one chest to drop your items, a lot of crafting mats have weight, alchemy is fine I guess, but you need a blacksmith to make weapons and armour and a trapper to make traps, to make things somehow worse the crafting menu is really bad, everything is clumped together, they didn't bother seperating them into lists, if you need to craft one specific thing get ready to spend ages looking through all your craftables- Also what kind of game makes you carry the crafting recipe on you in order to craft??!?!? genuine question does any other game force you to have them on you? Also the inventory is very annoying to use, I played on a controller so that might be the issue I suppose but in a game where the controller is an option it should be functional, if you want me to use a mouse and keyboard just say so.

[b] Unreponsiveness & Combat:[/b] Man, this game is really unresponsive, some buttons are responsive, your light attack, your parry, and your dodge are responsive, everything else is not, pressing heavy attack is really clunky, throwing a bomb or a dagger is really slow, and sometimes Geralt ignores your input entirely, heck the game is generally slow and I'm only now used to it, everything in this game takes a good 2 seconds longer than you expect it to, getting up from a knowck down takes forever, opening a door takes forever, there's an awkward split second after combat finishers, it's so strange, even when you approach a door or a ledge for example you'd assume the prompt to open it to instantly show up but no, it takes a good 3 or 4 seconds for it- even skipping the opening cinematic takes 4-6 seconds- Doesn't help when enemy attacks are janky and poorly telegraphed.

[b] Map:[/b] The map in this game is as useful as nipples on a crocodile, both Map and Minimap show whatever the ♥♥♥♥ they want, they're not accurate, they're not helpful, you're on your own, even the "quest goal" doesn't help you navigate, all it does is show you the general direction of your goal. (also there are random green/blue/red dots that show up on the map and they mean nothing.)

[b] Loc Muinne:[/b] Loc Muinne is the final chapter of this game and it can go to hell, the city is dull and ugly, very hard to navigate, and there's literally nothing going on in it, there's like 3-4 side quests and none of them are good.

[b] No Kiryu:[/b] This game does not feature Kiryu Kazuma from the hit series "Like A Dragon" :c

Thanks for reading my second-ever review, <3 and sorry for the bad English ;w; it's my second language!
Yeesh- got so tired of this game I need a bri'ish biscuit to calm me down.

Great followup to the first game, the story is great but the combat is much more shalow than in the first one. Personally it feels like that there is a lack of sidequest, but over all a very great game
Pretty buggy on my hardware, but it was still an enjoyable experience!

Definitely the worst entry of the trilogy, due to the arcade-like gameplay and it's general aim for a casual console audience, with a great storyline this is overall a solid game that i can recommend.
This was a great game no reason to skip it at all. I enjoyed the branching paths, definitely worth a second playthrough if your into that.

Despite struggling to get through doors for many hours, this is one of the best games ive every played.

Between story and the side quests - it was just the right amount, everything felt tied together by the end. 

The older style and feel to the game would typically turn people away, though I can honestly say I think it is part of the major charm of this game. 

The characters had great developement, npc's had incredible depth to them, the environments were incredibly detailed, especially within the towns and camps.

I came to this game from [i]The Witcher 3[/i] (which I loved) and found it… a bit rough.

The controls are awkward. Combat animations are long and uncancellable, and there's frustrations like the camera jumping around, and foreground objects obscuring my view of the battle. Press the 'sign' key (Q) and Geralt will draw whichever sword he had out last, which isn't necessarily the one you'll need to fight the enemy in front of you.

With the controls being so clunky, you'd expect the combat to be terrible. But no: it's actually loads of fun! Fights are fast and Geralt's satisfyingly quick and acrobatic. Using a sign at the right moment can tip a battle in your favor, but the brief casting time will also leave you exposed. You have to be somewhat strategic about whether to press your attacks, or back off and let your stamina regenerate. I had the most fun fighting big groups of enemies, where you have to frantically hack away, then dodge as enemies close in.

Like [i]TW3[/i], it's important to use power-ups such as potions and oils. Unfortunately, the game will often throw you into fights unexpectedly, so you don't get the chance to prepare. Potions can only be drunk while meditating, which is another little annoyance.

The locations you'll visit in the game are cool, but the exploration is rather disappointing. Not being able to jump or climb except in pre-determined spots, you're limited in how you can experience the world: it feels like a 2½-D game in a way. The developers could also have sprinkled some more loot or easter eggs around. A few times, I journeyed to some out-of-the-way part of the map, only to find… nothing. Bummer.

The UI and menus are as clumsy as the controls. Inventory and shop screens are at once cluttered and sparse: they're loaded with icons, but lack the information I [b]want[/b] to see when comparing weapons, managing item weight, etc.

The area map screen is decent enough, but lacks any sort of filtering, and many points of interest are unlabelled. For example, if you're trying to find your way back to a particular character's house, it can be difficult unless you happen to have a quest marker tagging it. And the game is stingy about these: many times, I found myself wandering around looking for this or that character. I had to consult walkthroughs occasionally when the game gave me [b]no[/b] clue where to go next.

Because this review is coming off more negative than I expected, let me mention some of the game's good qualities before I wrap it up:
- The graphics are beautiful
- Story is compelling
- Music is excellent throughout
- The game has two completely different Act 2s depending on the choices you make. Neat!
- The universe and cast of characters are solid

So in total, very much a mixed bag for me. While I've given it a reluctant thumbs up, the biggest impression that [i]The Witcher 2[/i] made on me was to instill even greater respect for its successor.

While I am biased and still like Witcher 1 more (The boomer-ass gameplay, terrible animations and the superb music) , Witcher 2 is a massive improvement over the previous formula in almost every way. 

I was honestly quite shocked to see how good this game looks. The Witcher 2 really proves the theory true that games have stagnated in terms of... well everything. Put the Witcher 2 up against ANY modern title, and chances are TW2 will beat it in some category which is wild for a game that is 15 years old. 

The Witcher 2 has great, believable voice acting (WAY better than the voice acting of Witcher 1, which was very cheesy and hilarious at times), excellent music, really good combat and a well-written intriguing storyline. Add to this some of the best sidequests an RPG has to offer and you have a thoroughly enjoyable 30-40 hour role-playing experience(Do not mind my hours played on my profile; I played this game a lot on the PC when it released on disc and also finished it a few times on the 360 which was obviously the most inferior way to play this game).

And choices actually matter. There is a point in the game where you have to decide whose side you are on and the decision you make actually leads to a completely different area, outcome and storyline for the rest of the game. So a person can actually get 60 hours of gameplay out of this if you decide to see where both story branches lead, which I think is definitely worth experiencing as almost every narrative that this game has to offer is bloody well worth it.

My only gripe with this game is the really bad difficulty balancing. The early hours of the game on Dark/Insane (Insane is permadeath mode) is really poor and probably the most frustrating as everything is overpowered until you manage to upgrade Quen and some of the passive abilities that allow you to survive cheapshots and arrows/bolts from ranged enemies.
The Witcher World isn't just a game's story it's another life for us
Big step up from one. Still very playable coming to it after playing 3. If you're a fan of the series, you definitely want to play this!

Positives:
- Great story on its own and a great sequel. I mention in my Witcher 1 review that these 2 are best played back-to-back if you decide to pick up 1, because this is where Geralt's memory begins to return and the space between *Season of Storms* (the final book of Sapkowski's main saga) and TW1 is filled. 
- Adding on to the previous point, you do not necessarily need to play 1 if you want to play this. The necessary information you need is provided fairly early on.
- Solid combat system. A bit repetitive and still somewhat janky, but it delves more into the realtime system that we eventually see in TW3. You truly get to see how Witchers fight and feel it with every strike, block, dodge, and Sign.
- Narrative structure: it fixes many of the problems that its predecessor has. The game is cut down to 3 semi-large chapters with a relatively-short Prologue and Epilogue. Your choices have much more weight compared to the original for Geralt's story & relationships and also the world itself.
- Excellent replayability. Similar to the original, the factions you side with determine how the story plays out, specifically if in Chapter 1 you decide to side with Temerian commando Vernon Roche or Scoia'tael commando Iorveth. Both of which have their own unique storylines that you follow based on that choice for the rest of the game.
- Adding onto this, something important to note is that the skill trees are greatly reduced to specifically Combat/Magic/Alchemy with an additional Witcher Training tree. While one might argue this is cutting down on the variability given in the original )trees for each Sign, etc.), I'd argue that the original (while surprisingly in-depth) suffers from just having too many trees to follow, making builds and playstyles rather confused. This trims the fat while still providing variability.
- Themes and world adherence; same praises I gave to the original.
- One more: WAY better characters. Not even close. Dynamics with returning and original characters are real and evolve in engaging ways as the story goes along.
Negatives:
- Gameplay is still janky and repetitive. Not nearly as atrocious as the original, but still something that can be a bit distracting particularly during boss fights or facing multiple opponents. I do find having the same structure of "3-4 contracts, gambling quest, arm-wrestling/fighting quest" to be a little bit annoying, although they're more fun to do due to the improvements made in this game.
- I was not a fan of only being able to drink potions while meditating. It wastes unnecessary time and makes combat/gameplay really frustrating, particularly when I can't drink a potion over a long stretch such as toward a Chapter's finale. I somewhat struggle to imagine what it must be like to do an Alchemist run because of that to an extent.
- The maps. Oh my god the maps. Again, they're more engaging and interesting to explore than the original's, but the novelty wears off very quickly once you enter a new area. After that, be ready for a lot of backtracking. And once again you won't get a horse until TW3 so get used to it.
Overall:
-Very good! 7.5/10 probably. Once again, CDPR thrives off its genuine passion for this IP and you can see the soul put into every nook and cranny. It makes a lot of quality of life improvements while also keeping the heart of the original and the heart of the novels it's based off of. The returning characters are all great, the original characters are very solid as well. It sets up its successor (TW3) excellently as well, regardless of the decisions you make, while also making sure your decisions still have an impact on the world.
- Would I recommend this? Absolutely. It's a much shorter commitment compared to The Witcher 3 so if you're not sure if you should play TW3, do try this first. No need to get TW1 to understand this though. :)

Ever since I finished the first Witcher, I was excited to jump into this one. I really enjoyed the story in the original, and I ended up playing through it twice for the achievements and to see the different paths the story could take. Seeing how much your choices matter was one of my favorite parts of the game.

My only real issue with the game is the combat. It's definitely a huge improvement over the first game, but it still feels clunky at times. Sometimes the responsiveness feels off, like Geralt doesn't react quite when you expect him to, and there were plenty of moments where I felt like I got hit when I shouldn't have. It's not bad, just awkward enough that it occasionally became frustrating.

The arm wrestling minigame was also bizarrely cursed for me because it absolutely tanked my FPS for some reason. No idea why. On the other hand, I actually enjoyed the dice poker minigame quite a bit.

The potion system is still a really interesting mechanic, and I like how preparation matters, but I found myself using the same few combinations over and over throughout the game. I kind of wish there had been more reasons to experiment with different brews.

Overall, I had a great time with it. The story, characters, and choice-driven quests easily carried the experience for me, and now that I've finished it, I can't wait to finally start The Witcher 3.
